enclosureTypes.name: Dome Closure
keyFeatures: Cylindrical or dome-shaped body with removable end cap
Gel or mechanical (compression or wedge) cable port sealing
Multiple fiber entry ports (4–8 ports typical)
Re-enterable without special tooling in most models
Stackable splice trays — easy to add capacity within the same closure
Accepts both loose-tube and tight-buffer cable types
enclosureTypes.fieldNotes: The most common OSP splice closure type. Suited for all installation environments. Gel-sealed versions are preferred for direct buried and high-water-table locations. Mechanical-seal and wedge-seal versions are cleaner for re-entry in vault and aerial work. Before ordering, size to cable count plus 20% spare tray capacity for future splices — undersized closures are a common field problem.

nerdStuff.title: Gel vs. Mechanical vs. Wedge Sealing
nerdStuff.explanation: Gel-sealed domes (FOSC-450 series) use a petroleum-based gel that fills all air gaps and cable entry points. Gel provides excellent moisture protection over decades but complicates re-entry (messy, cold-weather gel hardens) and may require a gel-cleaning kit. Mechanical-seal domes use compression fittings and O-rings — clean re-entry and proper torque on all ports is required for a valid seal. Wedge-seal systems (AFL Apex) use a tool-free push-to-lock wedge design — no gel, no heat shrink, no adhesives. Wedge sealing is the fastest for re-entry and is the current best practice for new installations where repeated access is expected.

enclosureTypes.name: Inline / Horizontal Closure
keyFeatures: Pass-through cable entry — the cable enters one end and exits the other without being cut
Clamshell or split-body design for quick tray access on a pole or strand
Strand mounting brackets or bail attachments for aerial lashing
Preserves cable strength member continuity through the closure
Horizontal splice tray orientation in most models
enclosureTypes.fieldNotes: Preferred for mid-span aerial applications where the cable continues through the closure. Only the needed fibers are broken out and spliced — the rest pass through untouched. Easier to open on a pole than a dome. Strand installation requires proper lashing hardware and load ratings — verify closure weight (full with trays and cable) against aerial span specifications.

enclosureTypes.name: Wall-Mount / Building Entry Terminal
keyFeatures: Fixed wall or backboard mounting
Indoor and weatherproof outdoor variants available
Often includes both splice tray slots and adapter panel (connector) positions
Accepts distribution cable on feeder side, drop cables on output side
MDU variants support FTTH drop cable slack storage
enclosureTypes.fieldNotes: Common at building entry points and MDU distribution heads. The indoor version provides organized cable management and port labeling. Outdoor weatherproof versions are IP-rated for exterior wall mounting. In MDU applications, locate the terminal in a lockable space — untrained residents will pull on fiber if it's accessible.

enclosureTypes.name: Rack-Mount Splice / Patch Enclosure
keyFeatures: 19-inch or 23-inch EIA rack compatible
Front-access trays in most designs — no need to pull the unit from the rack
Integrated cable routing with bend radius guides
Often combined with LC/SC/MPO adapter panel on the front face
High-density MPO/MTP modules available (72 or 144 fibers per 1U)
enclosureTypes.fieldNotes: Standard in central offices, equipment rooms, and data centers. The highest-density fiber termination option. Label every port before any fiber is terminated — in a 7-foot rack with 2000+ fibers, unlabeled ports will create permanent maintenance problems. Use patch cord management spools at every row.

enclosureTypes.name: Fiber Distribution Hub (FDH)
keyFeatures: Integrated PON splitter tray slots (1:4 to 1:64 PLC splitters)
Feeder cable input on one side, distribution and drop cable outputs on the other
Weatherproof housing rated for direct buried, aerial, or vault installation
Integrated patch panel for drop cable connections
Lockable — security-critical since the FDH serves multiple customers
enclosureTypes.fieldNotes: The FTTH/GPON distribution point where feeder fiber is split to customer drops. Size the splitter count conservatively — it is expensive to retrofit additional split capacity later. Document every port assignment (which port goes to which address) at time of installation. PON splitter insertion loss (≈17 dB for 1:64) is the largest single loss element in the GPON link budget.

nerdStuff.title: Optical Splitter Insertion Loss

formulas.name: Splitter Loss (theoretical)

variables.meaning: Split ratio — number of output ports

formulas.example: 1:32 splitter → 10 × log10(32) = 15.05 dB theoretical. Typical measured value: 16–17 dB including excess loss.

nerdStuff.explanation: Optical PLC splitters divide power equally across N output ports. Theoretical loss is 10×log(N). Actual measured loss is 1–2 dB higher due to excess loss in the planar lightwave circuit substrate. A 1:64 splitter has approximately 18 dB insertion loss — this single component consumes the majority of the GPON power budget (typically 28–32 dB total). Cable, splice, and connector losses must all fit within the remaining 10–14 dB margin.

notableModels.notes: The most commonly encountered dome closure in North American OSP — often called the 'Corning 450' in the field due to historical association with Siecor/Corning Cable Systems, but manufactured by CommScope (acquired from TE Connectivity/Raychem). D is the largest variant: 30" long × 11.5" diameter, 6 gel-sealed cable ports. Capacity: 768 single-fiber splices (with standard splice array trays) or 1728 fibers (standard 12-fiber ribbon) or 3456 fibers (rollable ribbon). The current industry workhorse for large-count feeder and backbone splices.

Mid-size variant: 23" long, 6 gel-sealed cable ports. Capacity: 288 single-fiber splices or 864 fibers (standard ribbon) or 1152 fibers (rollable ribbon). Common on distribution splices where the D model would be oversized.

Compact variant for access network tap-off and distribution splice points. Capacity: 144 single-fiber splices or 288–576 fibers (ribbon). Commonly found at feeder cable breakout points in residential OSP.

CommScope's FIST® trademarked closure line (not to be confused with AFL's FIST cable technology). Uses a UMS (Universal Modular System) tray design. GCO2 is available in round (B-series) and flat (F-series) body styles. Capacity up to 144 splices depending on configuration. Often specified for building entry and vault applications where the FOSC form factor is not needed.

brands.notes: CommScope owns the FOSC and FIST® product lines in North American OSP. The FOSC-450 series (originally Raychem, later Tyco Electronics/TE Connectivity) is the dominant gel-sealed dome closure in North America and is widely stocked by OSP contractors. Note: In the field, the FOSC-450 is frequently called a 'Corning closure' due to the historical Siecor/Corning Cable Systems connection — but the correct ordering source is CommScope. Always verify the 450-series tray part number before ordering replacements; tray incompatibility between closure variants is a common field procurement error.

notableModels.notes: AFL's mid-size OSP dome closure. 25" × 12" dimensions, 6 cable ports. Capacity: 576 single-fiber splices (standard trays) or 1152 fibers (standard 12-fiber ribbon) or 3456 fibers (SpiderWeb Ribbon / rollable ribbon). Uses AFL's wedge-based sealing system — no gel, no heat shrink, no adhesives. Tool-free re-entry. Widely specified by US Tier 1 carriers.

AFL's high-capacity OSP dome closure. Capacity: 864 single-fiber splices (standard trays) or 1296 fibers (standard 12-fiber ribbon) or 5148 fibers (SpiderWeb Ribbon / rollable ribbon). Same wedge-sealing system as X-2. Preferred for large-count feeder and backbone splice points where ribbon cable is in use.

AFL's compact OSP dome closure. Capacity: 144 single-fiber splices (standard trays) or 432 fibers (standard ribbon) or 864 fibers (rollable ribbon). Common for distribution tap-off and access splice points.

brands.notes: AFL (American Fiber Systems, affiliated with Fujikura) closures are specified by several major US carriers. The Apex series is the current product line for OSP dome closures. Note: AFL also uses 'FIST' as an acronym for their Fiber-in-Steel-Tube cable technology — this is completely separate from CommScope's FIST® closure product line. Do not confuse the two. AFL also manufactures fusion splicers under the Fujikura brand — common to see Apex closures used alongside Fujikura splicers on the same carrier projects.

notableModels.notes: PLP's standard dome closure for distribution OSP. Capacity: up to 288 single-fiber splices or 576 fibers (ribbon configuration). Known for robust sealing and straightforward field re-entry. One of the most common non-FOSC domes in North American OSP plant.

PLP's high-density dome closure: 13.33" diameter × 29.47" length — one of the largest dome closures available. Capacity: up to 1728 single-fiber splices (6 deep-profile trays) or 3456 fibers (12 thin-profile ribbon trays). Purpose-built for high-count feeder and backbone applications. Less common in distribution plant.

brands.notes: PLP is a significant North American OSP presence, particularly for aerial and direct-buried applications. Their hardware (strand clamps, cable lashers, anchors) is ubiquitous alongside fiber closures. The Coyote brand is well-regarded for field durability and wide compatibility with various cable types.

spliceTrays.name: 12-Splice Standard Tray
spliceTrays.notes: The common OSP splice tray form factor. Holds 12 fusion or mechanical splices in individual heat-shrink sleeve holders. Used in distribution and access closures. Not all 12-splice trays are cross-compatible between closure brands — verify tray part number against the closure series before ordering.

spliceTrays.name: CommScope 450-Series Splice Array Module
spliceTrays.notes: Proprietary splice array tray for FOSC-450 series closures (A, B, C, D variants). Each module holds up to 96 single-fiber splices in a high-density arrangement. 8 modules in a FOSC-450D = 768 total single-fiber splices. Not interchangeable with standard 12-splice generic trays. Part numbers differ between FOSC-450 variants — always specify the closure model when ordering replacement trays.

spliceTrays.name: 12-Ribbon Mass-Fusion Tray
spliceTrays.notes: Designed for mass-fusion ribbon splicing. Holds 12 ribbon splices × 12 fibers each = 144 fibers per tray. Used in high-count OSP (144F, 288F, 432F cables). Requires a ribbon-capable mass-fusion splicer — a single-fiber splicer cannot splice ribbons. Dramatically faster than individual fusion splicing at high fiber counts. Rollable ribbon (SpiderWeb Ribbon) trays can reach 288+ fibers per tray in compatible closures.

selectionGuide.scenario: Direct buried mid-span splice on distribution cable (24F–144F)
selectionGuide.notes: Dome with gel seal (FOSC-450B or C, or AFL Apex X-1) for maximum moisture protection. Size to cable fiber count plus 20% spare tray capacity. Mark closure location on as-built drawings — re-finding a buried closure without GPS coordinates is costly.

selectionGuide.scenario: Aerial mid-span splice on lashed ADSS or figure-8 cable
selectionGuide.notes: Inline horizontal for cable continuity through the closure. Strand-mount hardware required — verify closure weight (loaded) against aerial span design. Protect splice closure from traffic damage at pole level if near roadway.

selectionGuide.scenario: Underground vault splice on high-count feeder cable (288F+)
selectionGuide.notes: Large-capacity dome (FOSC-450D or AFL Apex X-3) with ribbon tray capability for mass-fusion efficiency. Mechanical or wedge seal for clean vault re-entry. Leave adequate slack coil inside the vault for future splicing.

selectionGuide.scenario: FTTH distribution point / PON splitter location
selectionGuide.notes: FDH with integrated splitter tray slots sized for target split ratio (1:32 or 1:64). Include 25% spare port capacity for new customer connects. Weatherproof rating appropriate for installation environment. Lockable enclosure required — security is critical.

selectionGuide.scenario: Building entry terminal / MDU fiber head-end
selectionGuide.notes: Wall-mount terminal with splice and patch panel capability. Indoor weatherproof (IP-rated) version if in unconditioned space. Locate in a secured equipment room or locked closet when possible.

selectionGuide.scenario: Central office or equipment room termination
selectionGuide.notes: Rack-mount for high-density termination. Use MPO/MTP modules for 72F or 144F per 1U. Label every port at installation — retrofitting labels on a populated rack is a multi-hour job.
